tool.label: Asset Allocator
tool.reason: How much of your money belongs outside India? Put in your numbers and see how big an international sleeve makes sense.

seo.title: The Rupee Keeps Falling… Should You Invest Abroad Too?
seo.description: In this clip from This or That with Bhartendra, Devina Mehra explains why Indian investors can’t afford to stay concentrated only in India. The rupee has

takeaways: The rupee has depreciated 90% against the dollar over a single career span, so wealth held only in rupees quietly loses global buying power over time.
India is less than 5% of global market cap, which means an India-only portfolio leaves out the vast majority of the world's markets.
Concentration in one country is a real risk, not safety: a single country crisis, like the Asian Crisis, can wipe out a net worth.
Hard-currency costs like foreign education, travel, and retirement abroad are rising, so a chunk of future spending will not be in rupees.
Global does not mean only the U.S.: themes rotate across countries and asset classes, and dedicated products now let Indian investors access true global diversification from under 9 lakh rupees.

fork.decision: Should you add global exposure to your portfolio, or keep everything invested in India?

this.label: Add global exposure
points: The rupee has depreciated 90% against the dollar over a career span, steadily eroding rupee-only wealth.
India is less than 5% of global market cap, so investing only at home ignores most of the world's opportunities.
One country's crisis, like the Asian Crisis, can wipe out a net worth that sits in a single market.

that.label: Stay India-only
points: Your income and everyday expenses are in rupees, so keeping money at home feels like the natural default.
True global diversification beyond the usual U.S. indices needed a dedicated product starting under 9 lakh rupees, making it less accessible than simply investing at home.

fork.pick: Add global exposure. With the rupee down 90% over a career and India under 5% of world market cap, holding everything in one country is a concentration risk, not safety. Spread across countries and asset classes rather than only U.S. indices, and know that going truly global can now start from under 9 lakh rupees.
